text.data-narration: Repeated games are a fundamental concept in algorithmic game theory, which studies the strategic interactions between rational players.

A repeated game is a sequence of staged games played between the same set of players over a finite or infinite number of periods. Each stage game is a finite game with a finite number of players, and the players' strategies are chosen for each stage game, taking into account the strategies of the other players.

g.g:
<text x="15" y="150" font-size="16" font-family="Arial" text-anchor="start" fill="#FFFFFF" data-narration="Repeated games have the following key characteristics:
Finite or infinite number of periods
Stage games
Strategic interactions

<text x="15" y="210" font-size="16" font-family="Arial" text-anchor="start" fill="#FFFFFF" data-narration="There are three types of repeated games:
Finite horizon
Infinite horizon
Zero-sum games

<text x="15" y="280" font-size="16" font-family="Arial" text-anchor="start" fill="#FFFFFF" data-narration="The following equilibrium concepts are used to analyze repeated games:
Nash Equilibrium
Pareto Optimum

<text x="15" y="350" font-size="16" font-family="Arial" text-anchor="start" fill="#FFFFFF" data-narration="The following strategies are used in repeated games:
Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium
Percivalian Equilibrium
